teamObjective.explanation: Partner has only 2 cards and is close to finishing, so I want to lead a low single that they can realistically beat, giving them control to play out their remaining cards.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner likely holds at least one card strong enough to beat a low diamond single, given they are down to 2 cards, but their exact ranks and suits are unknown.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents each have 3 cards and will try to take the trick to deny my partner the lead; they may hold trump, the rank-7, or simply higher non-trump cards, but no specific cards are known.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the diamond-3 sets the lowest possible single, giving partner the easiest chance to win the trick while keeping my higher diamonds (4, 5, 6) for later rounds.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The 6 is my highest non-trump card; leading it wastes a resource and raises the bar partner must clear to take the lead.
Slightly higher than the 3 and therefore makes it marginally harder for partner to win without saving a noticeably stronger card.
Intermediate between 3 and 6; less conservative than leading the 3 and does not add enough strength to justify giving up the lowest lead.

risks: An opponent may have a higher single or trump and capture the lead immediately.
Partner may not have a card that beats the lead, so the lead-transfer plan could fail.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By leading the smallest card I preserve the rest of my hand and accept the uncertainty inherent in the opening lead; I can use any retained higher diamond in a later trick if I regain the lead.
